text:
CT NECK WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort, odynophagia, referred otalgia, and a firm non-tender left level II cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2.5 cm on examination. Persistent symptoms despite a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate. Evaluate for underlying mass or lymphadenopathy. History of hypothyroidism and GERD. No prior head and neck imaging on file for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the neck was performed from the skull base through the thoracic inlet using a helical acquisition technique before and after intravenous administration of iohexol contrast (100 mL at 3 mL/sec via right antecubital vein). Images were acquired in the axial plane at 0.625 mm slice thickness and reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes with soft tissue and bone algorithm kernels. No adverse reaction to contrast. Limited chest windows were obtained through the lung apices and upper mediastinum.

FINDINGS:

Primary Site - Base of Tongue / Oropharynx:
There is an ill-defined but discernible soft tissue mass measuring approximately 2.2 cm in greatest dimension (AP x transverse x craniocaudal: 2.2 x 1.9 x 2.0 cm) centered at the left base of tongue. The mass demonstrates avid and heterogeneous enhancement following contrast administration, with slightly irregular margins. It arises from the left glossotonsillar sulcus region and extends anteriorly and inferiorly toward the left vallecula. The epiglottis is displaced but appears intact without definite direct invasion. There is asymmetric fullness and thickening of the left base of tongue mucosa relative to the right side. The lingual tonsil tissue on the right appears normal. The oropharyngeal airway is not significantly compromised. Pre-epiglottic space appears preserved. No definite involvement of the posterior pharyngeal wall or piriform sinus.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
There are several enlarged lymph nodes in the left cervical chain at levels II and IIA with imaging features highly concerning for metastatic involvement. The dominant node in the left level IIA region measures 2.7 cm in short axis diameter and demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with a large central area of low density consistent with central liquefactive necrosis. This degree of central breakdown in the absence of prior treatment is a well-established morphologic feature of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma and is not expected in reactive or infectious adenopathy. Surrounding the dominant necrotic node, there are two to three additional mildly enlarged but non-necrotic nodes in the left level II region, the largest measuring approximately 1.3 cm in short axis diameter, with borderline preservation of the fatty hilum. There are no matted or confluent nodal conglomerates. No frank extranodal soft tissue extension is appreciated on the current study, though the dominant necrotic node abuts the posterior belly of the left digastric muscle with loss of the intervening fat plane over a short segment, which may warrant further characterization.

The right cervical chain levels I through VI demonstrates no enlarged, morphologically suspicious, or necrotic lymph nodes. The bilateral retropharyngeal nodal stations appear normal. The bilateral posterior cervical triangles (level V) are clear. Left level IB and level III nodes are within normal size limits with no internal necrosis.

Salivary Glands:
The bilateral parotid glands are symmetric, homogeneous, and without focal mass lesion, ductal dilatation, or calculi. The submandibular glands are symmetric and unremarkable bilaterally. No sialolithiasis. No intraductal obstruction.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is normal in size and morphology. There is a subcentimeter (5 mm) hypodense nodule within the right thyroid lobe, appearing simple and without suspicious features (no microcalcifications, no hypervascularity). This likely represents a small colloid cyst or benign thyroid nodule. No dominant thyroid mass. No thyroid enlargement or goiter. No suspicious cervical thyroid tissue.

Larynx and Hypopharynx:
The laryngeal architecture is preserved. The true and false vocal cords appear symmetric and normally positioned. The arytenoids and aryepiglottic folds are symmetric. Piriform sinuses are symmetric and patent. No subglottic extension or cricoid involvement. No pre-epiglottic or paraglottic fat infiltration.

Vascular Structures:
The bilateral common, internal, and external carotid arteries are patent and demonstrate normal caliber and enhancement. No carotid encasement or displacement by the dominant necrotic lymph node, though medial nodal position places the node in proximity to the left internal jugular vein, which appears patent. No venous thrombosis.

Cervical Soft Tissues and Bones:
The strap musculature and platysma are symmetric. No prevertebral soft tissue thickening. The visualized cervical vertebral bodies demonstrate no aggressive osseous lesions. No fracture or malalignment.

Chest Windows (Limited Survey through Upper Thorax):
The lung apices and upper mediastinum are included on the acquisition. No pulmonary nodules or masses are identified in the visualized lung parenchyma. No hilar or mediastinal adenopathy. No pleural effusion. Osseous structures of the upper thorax appear unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Enhancing soft tissue mass measuring approximately 2.2 cm at the left base of tongue, extending toward the left vallecula, with morphologic features highly suspicious for a primary malignant neoplasm. In the clinical context of a 52-year-old with odynophagia, left neck mass, and referred otalgia, squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx is the foremost diagnostic consideration. Direct tissue sampling is strongly warranted.

2. Dominant left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.7 cm with extensive central necrosis and peripheral rim enhancement, accompanied by additional mildly enlarged left level II lymph nodes. This constellation is strongly suggestive of metastatic cervical lymphadenopathy, most consistent with regional nodal spread from an oropharyngeal primary. Possible minimal loss of fat plane adjacent to the left digastric muscle is noted but extranodal extension cannot be definitively confirmed or excluded on this study.

3. No contralateral cervical lymphadenopathy. No retropharyngeal or posterior triangle nodal involvement.

4. No evidence of distant metastatic disease on the limited chest survey included. Dedicated chest and staging imaging with PET-CT would be appropriate following tissue confirmation.

5. Small right thyroid nodule (5 mm), likely benign. May be noted at follow-up per thyroid nodule guidelines, low clinical priority in this setting.

Communicated to referring otolaryngology service.
